## Brute-force protection (fail2ban) [#brute-force-protection-fail2ban]

`fail2ban` is a watchful little daemon that tails the SSH log and
bans IP addresses that rack up failed logins. On Trixie there's a
wrinkle: `rsyslog` is no longer installed by default, so `fail2ban`
has to read authentication events straight from the systemd journal.
Sounds fiddly, but it's two extra pieces and you're done.

1. Install `fail2ban` and the Python bindings for the systemd journal:

   ```bash
   sudo apt install fail2ban python3-systemd
   ```

2. Create a local configuration that uses the journal backend:

   ```bash
   sudo nano /etc/fail2ban/jail.local
   ```

   Paste:

   ```ini
   [DEFAULT]
   backend = systemd
   bantime = 10m
   findtime = 10m
   maxretry = 5

   [sshd]
   enabled = true
   ```

3. Restart `fail2ban` and confirm the SSH jail is running:

   ```bash
   sudo systemctl restart fail2ban
   sudo fail2ban-client status sshd
   ```

   Expected output:

   ```text
   Status for the jail: sshd
   |- Filter
   |  |- Currently failed: 0
   |  |- Total failed:     0
   |  `- Journal matches:  _SYSTEMD_UNIT=sshd.service + _COMM=sshd
   `- Actions
      |- Currently banned: 0
      |- Total banned:     0
      `- Banned IP list:
   ```

<Callout type="info" title="Why not the default backend?">
  Stock Debian `fail2ban` assumes `rsyslog` is writing `/var/log/auth.log`.
  On Trixie it isn't, skip the two changes above and `fail2ban` will
  run happily, read an empty log, and ban precisely nobody.
</Callout>

**Main takeaway:** any IP that fumbles five logins gets a
ten-minute timeout, automatically, in the background, forever.

## Raise the open-files limit [#raise-the-open-files-limit]

Under heavy load, or a deliberate flood, `bitcoind` and LND open a
lot of TCP connections at once. The default file-handle limit is far
too low for that and throws `too many open files` errors.

1. Create `/etc/security/limits.d/90-limits.conf`:

   ```bash
   sudo nano /etc/security/limits.d/90-limits.conf
   ```

   Paste:

   ```text
   *    soft nofile 128000
   *    hard nofile 128000
   root soft nofile 128000
   root hard nofile 128000
   ```

2. Make sure PAM actually applies those limits on login. Open each
   of these files and add a `pam_limits.so` line, right before any
   trailing comment:

   ```bash
   sudo nano /etc/pam.d/common-session
   sudo nano /etc/pam.d/common-session-noninteractive
   ```

   Add:

   ```text
   session required                        pam_limits.so
   ```

The new limits kick in at the next login.

## Disable Bluetooth and Wi-Fi [#disable-bluetooth-and-wi-fi]

A node dedicated to Bitcoin has no use for Bluetooth, and if you're
connected over Ethernet, no use for Wi-Fi either. Turning off the
radios you aren't using removes another slice of attack surface.

1. Edit the firmware configuration:

   ```bash test:skip
   sudo nano /boot/firmware/config.txt
   ```

2. Disable Bluetooth by adding this line at the end of the file:

   ```text
   dtoverlay=disable-bt
   ```

3. If the Pi is connected by Ethernet cable, also disable Wi-Fi:

   ```text
   dtoverlay=disable-wifi
   ```

Save and exit. The radios stay off from the next reboot onward.


# System configuration (/docs/raspberry-pi/system-configuration)



Fresh install, fresh habits. You'll patch the system, make sure the
SSD is pulling its weight, carve out a tidy place for all the node
data to live, and confirm that swap is doing its job. Quick pages,
all of it, but it's the groundwork the rest of the guide leans on.

## Log back in [#log-back-in]

Open an SSH session to the Pi as the `admin` user:

```bash
ssh admin@raspibolt.local
```

To end a session at any time, type `exit`. And a quick reminder:
anything that touches system files needs `sudo` in front of it
(the Pi will ask for &#x2A;*password \[A]** the first time each session).

## System update [#system-update]

Keeping the system patched is table stakes. Debian's `apt` package
manager handles OS updates and application upgrades in the same
command.

1. Set a default locale. This silences the noisy `LC_ALL: cannot
   change locale` warnings that some tools throw when you SSH in from
   a machine with a different language setting:

   ```bash
   echo "export LC_ALL=C" >> ~/.bashrc
   source ~/.bashrc
   ```

2. Refresh the package index and install every available upgrade in
   one go:

   ```bash
   sudo apt update && sudo apt full-upgrade
   ```

3. Install `git`, you'll need it later to clone source code:

   ```bash
   sudo apt install git
   ```

<Callout type="info">
  Run `sudo apt update && sudo apt full-upgrade` every few months to
  pick up security fixes. Set a reminder if you're the forgetful type.
</Callout>

## Check SSD performance [#check-ssd-performance]

A fast SSD is non-negotiable. A slow drive drags the initial block
download into the weekend and turns the Electrs index build into a
slog. This is why it pays to benchmark now, before you've committed
a few hundred gigabytes of blockchain data to the wrong enclosure.
The Pi 5 handles USB 3 storage well, but some enclosures still
underperform, this is the moment to catch that.

1. Install `hdparm`, a classic disk benchmarking tool:

   ```bash
   sudo apt install hdparm
   ```

2. Confirm your SSD shows up as `/dev/sda`:

   ```bash
   lsblk -pli
   ```

3. Measure raw read throughput:

   ```bash
   sudo hdparm -t --direct /dev/sda
   ```

   Expected output (your number will differ):

   ```text
   Timing O_DIRECT disk reads: 932 MB in  3.00 seconds = 310.23 MB/sec
   ```

Anything above **100 MB/s** is fine for a full node. If you see
numbers below 50 MB/s, the enclosure is probably falling back to UAS
mode with a buggy driver, a well-known problem with certain chipsets
(hello again, JMicron). The
[Fix bad USB3 performance](../troubleshooting#fix-bad-usb3-performance)
section in Troubleshooting walks through disabling UAS for a specific
drive.

<Callout type="info" title="Using NVMe via the M.2 HAT+">
  On NVMe the device is `/dev/nvme0n1`, not `/dev/sda`. You can skip
  this check entirely, NVMe drives comfortably clear any throughput
  bar this guide sets.
</Callout>

## Data directory [#data-directory]

Everything the node cares about, the blockchain, Lightning channel
state, backups, will live under `/data/`. Keeping it outside any
user's home directory makes permissions obvious and means you can
move it to a bigger drive later with nothing more than a mount.

Create it once, owned by `admin`:

```bash
sudo mkdir /data
sudo chown admin:admin /data
```

## Swap [#swap]

Swap is the safety net: when RAM fills up, the system offloads idle
pages to "swap" instead of crashing. Compressed swap held in RAM
sounds like a contradiction, but that's exactly what zram is, and
it works beautifully for a node. On Raspberry Pi OS Trixie this is
handled out of the box by the &#x2A;*`rpi-swap`** service, which uses
**zram** rather than a file on the SSD. Clever, and already running.

But you do **not** need to install `dphys-swapfile` or edit
`/etc/dphys-swapfile`. Old guides told you to; ignore them.

Verify that swap is active:

```bash
swapon --show
```

You should see something like:

```text
NAME       TYPE       SIZE USED PRIO
/dev/zram0 partition    2G   0B  100
```

On an 8 GB Pi 5, the default 2 GB zram swap is plenty.

<Callout type="info" title="Why zram and not a swapfile?">
  A swapfile on an SSD wears the flash with every heavy swap burst,
  and on a microSD card it's painfully slow. `zram` sidesteps both:
  pages are compressed and kept in RAM, so an 8 GB Pi effectively gets
  a little more memory without ever touching the disk.
</Callout>


# JAM (JoinMarket UI) (/docs/bonus/bitcoin/Jam)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/bitcoin/Jam.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[JAM](https://github.com/joinmarket-webui/jam) is a web interface for
JoinMarket that aims to make CoinJoin accessible to non-expert users.
It ships sensible defaults and a browser-based UI while still
exposing the advanced knobs experienced JoinMarket users rely on.


# UTXOracle (/docs/bonus/bitcoin/UTXOracle)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/bitcoin/UTXOracle.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[UTXOracle](https://utxo.live/oracle) is a Python script by Daniel Hinton
and Steve Jeffress that derives a close BTC/USD price estimate directly
from on-chain data, without talking to any exchange or external API.
It works because a predictable share of transaction outputs land on
round-dollar amounts, so the blockchain itself carries enough signal
to reconstruct a price.


# Bisq (/docs/bonus/bitcoin/bisq)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/bitcoin/bisq.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[Bisq](https://bisq.network/) is a decentralized bitcoin exchange, a
desktop application that offers a private, censorship-resistant way
to swap bitcoin for national currencies and other cryptocurrencies
over the internet. Pointing Bisq at your own node keeps transaction
lookups off third-party servers.


# BitBoxApp (/docs/bonus/bitcoin/bitboxapp)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/bitcoin/bitboxapp.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

The [BitBoxApp](https://shiftcrypto.ch/app/) is the beginner-friendly
companion app to the BitBox02 hardware wallet from Shift Crypto. By
configuring it to connect directly to your RaspiBolt instead of
Shift's servers, you verify your own transactions and avoid leaking
wallet addresses to a third party.


# Samourai Dojo (/docs/bonus/bitcoin/dojo)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/bitcoin/dojo.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[Samourai Dojo](https://code.samourai.io/dojo/samourai-dojo) is the
backing server for Samourai Wallet. It exposes HD account and BIP47
balance and transaction data, serves unspent output lists, and
broadcasts transactions through your own `bitcoind`, removing the
reliance on Samourai's hosted servers.


# Electrum Personal Server (/docs/bonus/bitcoin/electrum-personal-server)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/bitcoin/electrum-personal-server.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[Electrum Personal Server](https://github.com/chris-belcher/electrum-personal-server)
is an alternative to Electrs and Fulcrum that only indexes the
addresses in your own wallets rather than the entire chain. It uses
far less disk space at the cost of advance wallet registration and
slower lookups for new addresses.


# Electrum Wallet Desktop (/docs/bonus/bitcoin/electrum-wallet-desktop)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/bitcoin/electrum-wallet-desktop.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[Electrum Wallet](https://electrum.org) is a well-established,
feature-rich Bitcoin wallet for power users with broad hardware
wallet support. Pointing it at your own Electrs or Fulcrum server
keeps address lookups private and removes the dependency on public
Electrum servers.


# Fulcrum (/docs/bonus/bitcoin/fulcrum)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/bitcoin/fulcrum.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[Fulcrum](https://github.com/cculianu/Fulcrum) is a fast SPV server
for Bitcoin, Bitcoin Cash, and Litecoin written in C++ by Calin
Culianu. Independent benchmarks rank it as one of the quickest
Electrum server implementations available, which makes it a solid
alternative to Electrs when you want lower query latency.


# I2P (/docs/bonus/bitcoin/i2p)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/bitcoin/i2p.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[I2P](https://geti2p.net/en/), the Invisible Internet Protocol, is
an anonymous, end-to-end encrypted network layer where peers never
see each other's real IP addresses. The guide installs
[i2pd](https://i2pd.readthedocs.io/), a C++ I2P daemon, so Bitcoin
Core can reach peers over I2P in addition to clearnet and Tor.


# Bitcoin bonus (/docs/bonus/bitcoin)



This section covers Bitcoin tooling that goes beyond the core
RaspiBolt setup. You'll find self-hosted block explorers, desktop
and CLI wallets, alternative Electrum server implementations,
privacy layers like JoinMarket and I2P, and a testnet variant of
the whole stack. Pick the pieces that match how you use your node.

<Callout type="info" title="Content being migrated">
  The bonus pages are still being rewritten for v4. Each page links to
  the v3 source while we work through the backlog.
</Callout>

## What's in this section [#whats-in-this-section]

<Cards>
  <Card title="Mempool" href="/docs/bonus/bitcoin/mempool">
    Self-hosted Bitcoin blockchain and mempool visualizer.
  </Card>

  <Card title="UTXOracle" href="/docs/bonus/bitcoin/UTXOracle">
    Estimate the BTC/USD price from your own blockchain data.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Download the white paper" href="/docs/bonus/bitcoin/white-paper">
    Reconstruct the Bitcoin white paper PDF from on-chain data.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Bisq" href="/docs/bonus/bitcoin/bisq">
    Decentralized desktop exchange for non-KYC bitcoin.
  </Card>

  <Card title="BitBoxApp" href="/docs/bonus/bitcoin/bitboxapp">
    Beginner-friendly companion app for the BitBox02.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Electrum Wallet Desktop" href="/docs/bonus/bitcoin/electrum-wallet-desktop">
    Feature-rich desktop wallet for power users.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Specter Desktop" href="/docs/bonus/bitcoin/specter-desktop">
    Bitcoin Core GUI focused on multisig with hardware wallets.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Sparrow Terminal" href="/docs/bonus/bitcoin/sparrow-terminal">
    Headless Sparrow for systems without a display.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Samourai Dojo" href="/docs/bonus/bitcoin/dojo">
    Personal backend for Samourai Wallet.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Electrum Personal Server" href="/docs/bonus/bitcoin/electrum-personal-server">
    Lightweight Electrum server that indexes only your wallets.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Fulcrum" href="/docs/bonus/bitcoin/fulcrum">
    Fast Electrum-compatible SPV server, alternative to Electrs.
  </Card>

  <Card title="JoinMarket" href="/docs/bonus/bitcoin/joinmarket">
    Decentralized marketplace for CoinJoin transactions.
  </Card>

  <Card title="JAM (JoinMarket UI)" href="/docs/bonus/bitcoin/Jam">
    Friendly web interface for JoinMarket.
  </Card>

  <Card title="I2P" href="/docs/bonus/bitcoin/i2p">
    Anonymous network layer alongside Tor for Bitcoin Core.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Safu Ninja" href="/docs/bonus/bitcoin/safu-ninja">
    DIY steel backup for your seed phrases.
  </Card>

  <Card title="RaspiBolt on testnet" href="/docs/bonus/bitcoin/testnet">
    Run the full stack on testnet for safe experimentation.
  </Card>
</Cards>


# JoinMarket (/docs/bonus/bitcoin/joinmarket)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/bitcoin/joinmarket.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[JoinMarket](https://github.com/JoinMarket-Org/joinmarket-clientserver)
is a decentralized marketplace for CoinJoin transactions. Running it
on your node lets you either mix your own coins by paying a small
fee or become a liquidity maker and earn sats for providing the
inputs others use to mix.


# Mempool Explorer (/docs/bonus/bitcoin/mempool)



[Mempool](https://github.com/mempool/mempool) is the same open-source
code that runs mempool.space, self-hosted on your own node. Transaction
lookups, fee estimates, and UTXO queries stay on your LAN and never
reach a third party.

This guide builds Mempool v3.3.1 from source. The backend
stores indexed data in MariaDB and serves the API on port 8999. Caddy
puts the Angular frontend behind HTTPS on port 4080.

## Requirements [#requirements]

* [Electrum server](/docs/bitcoin/electrum-server) running (Electrs on port 50001)
* Node.js 22 installed (from the [BTC RPC Explorer guide](/docs/bitcoin/blockchain-explorer#install-nodejs-22-lts) or standalone via `nodesource.com`)
* Caddy installed (from the same guide or standalone via `caddyserver.com`)
* \~5 GB free on the SSD: build artefacts under `/home/mempool/` take 4-5 GB; the MariaDB database starts small but
  grows to \~5 GB with full historical address indexing enabled

## Create the user [#create-the-user]

A dedicated system user limits the blast radius of any bug. Adding it
to the `bitcoin` group lets Mempool authenticate to Bitcoin Core via
the cookie file, the same approach BTC RPC Explorer uses.

```bash
sudo adduser --disabled-password --gecos "" mempool
sudo adduser mempool bitcoin
```

## Install build dependencies [#install-build-dependencies]

Mempool's backend includes a Rust-native block-template builder (GBT).
Debian 13 Trixie ships Rust 1.85, which meets the 1.84 requirement:

```bash
sudo apt install rustc cargo
```

## Set up MariaDB [#set-up-mariadb]

```bash
sudo apt install mariadb-server
```

Create the database and a dedicated user. Replace `YourPasswordM` with
your &#x2A;*password \[M]** (new, used only for MariaDB here):

```bash
sudo mysql <<'SQL'
CREATE DATABASE mempool;
GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON mempool.* TO 'mempool'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY 'YourPasswordM';
SQL
```

## Install Mempool [#install-mempool]

Clone and check out the release as the `mempool` user:

```bash
sudo -u mempool bash <<'EOF'
git clone https://github.com/mempool/mempool ~/mempool
cd ~/mempool
git checkout v3.3.1
EOF
```

### Configure the backend [#configure-the-backend]

Write the config file. Replace `YourPasswordM` with your &#x2A;*password \[M]**:

```bash
sudo -u mempool tee /home/mempool/mempool/backend/mempool-config.json > /dev/null <<'CONFIG'
{
  "MEMPOOL": {
    "NETWORK": "mainnet",
    "BACKEND": "electrum",
    "HTTP_PORT": 8999,
    "SPAWN_CLUSTER_PROCS": 0,
    "API_URL_PREFIX": "/api/v1/",
    "POLL_RATE_MS": 2000,
    "CACHE_DIR": "./cache",
    "CACHE_ENABLED": true,
    "CLEAR_PROTECTION_MINUTES": 20,
    "RECOMMENDED_FEE_PERCENTILE": 50,
    "BLOCK_WEIGHT_UNITS": 4000000,
    "INITIAL_BLOCKS_AMOUNT": 8,
    "MEMPOOL_BLOCKS_AMOUNT": 8,
    "INDEXING_BLOCKS_AMOUNT": 0,
    "AUDIT": false,
    "RUST_GBT": true,
    "CPFP_INDEXING": false,
    "ALLOW_UNREACHABLE": true,
    "PRICE_UPDATES_PER_HOUR": 1
  },
  "CORE_RPC": {
    "HOST": "127.0.0.1",
    "PORT": 8332,
    "COOKIE": true,
    "COOKIE_PATH": "/data/bitcoin/.cookie"
  },
  "ELECTRUM": {
    "HOST": "127.0.0.1",
    "PORT": 50001,
    "TLS_ENABLED": false
  },
  "DATABASE": {
    "ENABLED": true,
    "HOST": "127.0.0.1",
    "PORT": 3306,
    "DATABASE": "mempool",
    "USERNAME": "mempool",
    "PASSWORD": "YourPasswordM"
  },
  "SOCKS5PROXY": {
    "ENABLED": false
  },
  "STATISTICS": {
    "ENABLED": true,
    "TX_PER_SECOND_SAMPLE_PERIOD": 150
  }
}
CONFIG
```

`INDEXING_BLOCKS_AMOUNT: 0` disables historical address indexing on
first run. Once the node is stable, raise it to `4320` (30 days) or
`11000` (about 3 months) to unlock address balance history in the UI.
Restart the service after changing it.

### Build the backend [#build-the-backend]

<Callout type="info" title="Build time: 10-20 minutes">
  `npm install` triggers a Rust compilation before installing Node.js packages.
  On a Pi 5 that takes 5-10 minutes. Run the commands in a `tmux` or
  `screen` session.
</Callout>

```bash
sudo su - mempool
cd ~/mempool/backend
npm install --prod
npm run build
exit
```

### Build the frontend [#build-the-frontend]

<Callout type="info" title="Build time: 20-30 minutes">
  Run in the same `tmux` or `screen` session. A dropped SSH connection
  would abort the build.
</Callout>

```bash
sudo su - mempool
cd ~/mempool/frontend
npm install
npm run generate-themes
npm run generate-config
npm run ng -- build --configuration production
npm run sync-assets
exit
```

The built frontend lands in `~/mempool/frontend/dist/mempool/browser`.

<Callout type="info" title="Why not npm run build?">
  The standard build script compiles all 33 translation locales, which
  takes several hours on a Pi. The commands above build only the English
  source locale and produce the same result.
</Callout>

## Autostart on boot [#autostart-on-boot]

Back as user `admin`, create the service file:

```bash
sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/mempool.service
```

Paste:

```ini
# RaspiBolt: systemd unit for Mempool
# /etc/systemd/system/mempool.service

[Unit]
Description=Mempool Bitcoin Explorer
After=network.target bitcoind.service electrs.service mariadb.service

[Service]
WorkingDirectory=/home/mempool/mempool/backend
ExecStart=/usr/bin/node --max-old-space-size=2048 dist/index.js
User=mempool
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=60
PrivateTmp=true
ProtectSystem=full
NoNewPrivileges=true
PrivateDevices=true

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
```

Enable and start:

```bash
sudo systemctl enable mempool
sudo systemctl start mempool
sudo journalctl -f -u mempool
```

Wait for a line containing `Listening on http://127.0.0.1:8999/`. On
first start, Mempool syncs its database from Electrs and Bitcoin Core.
Expect 30-60 minutes before fee charts and address lookups populate
fully.

Press `Ctrl`-`C` to stop following the log.

## HTTPS on the LAN with Caddy [#https-on-the-lan-with-caddy]

Add a Mempool block to the Caddyfile (create the file first if Caddy
was not already installed from the BTC RPC Explorer guide):

1. Open the Caddyfile:

   ```bash
   sudo nano /etc/caddy/Caddyfile
   ```

2. Add the Mempool block after your existing blocks:

   ```text
   # Mempool Explorer
   :4080 {
     tls internal

     handle /api/* {
       reverse_proxy localhost:8999
     }

     handle {
       root * /home/mempool/mempool/frontend/dist/mempool/browser
       try_files {path} /index.html
       file_server
     }
   }
   ```

   The `/api/*` block proxies API calls to the backend. Everything
   else is served as a static file, falling back to `index.html` so
   Angular's client-side routing works for deep links.

3. Open the firewall and reload Caddy:

   ```bash
   sudo ufw allow 4080/tcp comment 'Mempool Explorer (Caddy)'
   sudo systemctl reload caddy
   ```

4. Point your browser at `https://raspibolt.local:4080`. Accept the
   one-time certificate warning.

**Main takeaway:** you now have a private Bitcoin block explorer and
mempool visualizer. The node's own Electrs index answers every query.

## Upgrade [#upgrade]

Read the [release notes](https://github.com/mempool/mempool/releases)
before upgrading; some releases include a database migration.

```bash
sudo systemctl stop mempool
```

Switch to the `mempool` user and update the source:

```bash
sudo su - mempool
cd ~/mempool
git fetch
git checkout v<new-version>
exit
```

Rebuild the backend:

```bash
sudo su - mempool
cd ~/mempool/backend
npm install --prod
npm run build
exit
```

Rebuild the frontend:

```bash
sudo su - mempool
cd ~/mempool/frontend
npm install
npm run generate-themes
npm run generate-config
npm run ng -- build --configuration production
npm run sync-assets
exit
```

Start the service and confirm it's running:

```bash
sudo systemctl start mempool
sudo journalctl -u mempool --since "5 minutes ago"
```

## Uninstall [#uninstall]

```bash
sudo systemctl stop mempool
sudo systemctl disable mempool
sudo rm /etc/systemd/system/mempool.service
sudo ufw delete "allow 4080/tcp"
```

Remove the Mempool block from `/etc/caddy/Caddyfile`, then reload:

```bash
sudo systemctl reload caddy
```

Drop the database and user:

```bash
sudo mysql <<'SQL'
DROP DATABASE mempool;
DROP USER 'mempool'@'localhost';
SQL
```

If MariaDB is no longer needed:

```bash
sudo apt remove --purge mariadb-server mariadb-client
sudo apt autoremove
```

Remove the user and home directory:

```bash
sudo deluser mempool
sudo rm -rf /home/mempool
```


# Safu Ninja (/docs/bonus/bitcoin/safu-ninja)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/bitcoin/safu-ninja.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[Safu Ninja](https://safu.ninja/) is a DIY method for building
fire- and water-resistant steel backups of your mnemonic seed
phrases. It uses only off-the-shelf components, which means no
third-party vendor ever learns you own a hardware wallet or what
the device is guarding.


# Sparrow Terminal (/docs/bonus/bitcoin/sparrow-terminal)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/bitcoin/sparrow-terminal.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

Sparrow Terminal is a stripped-down build of [Sparrow Wallet](https://sparrowwallet.com/)
that runs on systems without a graphical display. It's primarily a
configuration utility for running Sparrow as a server daemon on your
node, with the GUI client connecting from another machine on your
network.


# Specter Desktop (/docs/bonus/bitcoin/specter-desktop)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/bitcoin/specter-desktop.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[Specter Desktop](https://specter.solutions/) wraps Bitcoin Core with
a convenient GUI and is purpose-built around multisignature setups
using hardware wallets and airgapped devices. It's a strong choice
when you want self-custody of larger amounts with a second-factor
signing device.


# RaspiBolt on testnet (/docs/bonus/bitcoin/testnet)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/bitcoin/testnet.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

Running a testnet RaspiBolt lets you explore Bitcoin and Lightning
with play-money coins. The guide highlights every configuration
difference from the mainnet setup so you can build or test tools and
integrations without the risk of losing real sats.


# Download the white paper (/docs/bonus/bitcoin/white-paper)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/bitcoin/white-paper.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

The Bitcoin white paper was embedded in the blockchain in April 2013
inside block 230,009. This guide walks through extracting and
reassembling the PDF from your own verified blockchain data, a nice
demonstration that the foundational document of Bitcoin is, quite
literally, part of Bitcoin itself.


# AmbossPing (/docs/bonus/lightning/ambossping)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/lightning/ambossping.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

AmbossPing is a small bash script that pings
[amboss.space](https://amboss.space) on a schedule to keep your node's
monitoring status fresh. Link your Telegram account to Amboss and
you'll receive push alerts whenever the ping status changes, without
needing ThunderHub or a heavier dashboard.


# Balance of Satoshis (/docs/bonus/lightning/balance-of-satoshis)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/lightning/balance-of-satoshis.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[Balance of Satoshis](https://github.com/alexbosworth/balanceofsatoshis)
(BoS) is a CLI toolkit for LND from Alex Bosworth. Its rebalancing
command is the headline feature, but BoS bundles dozens of other
utilities, node monitoring, Telegram bot integration, payment
routing inspection, that extend what you can do beyond `lncli`.


# charge-lnd (/docs/bonus/lightning/charge-lnd)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/lightning/charge-lnd.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[charge-lnd](https://github.com/accumulator/charge-lnd) is a simple,
policy-based fee manager for LND. You write rules matching groups
of channels (by peer, liquidity ratio, or activity) and charge-lnd
applies the matching fee rate on a schedule, so you stop tweaking
channel fees by hand.


# Circuit Breaker (/docs/bonus/lightning/circuit-breaker)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/lightning/circuit-breaker.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[Circuit Breaker](https://github.com/lightningequipment/circuitbreaker)
is a rate-limiting firewall for LND that protects against
HTLC-flooding griefing attacks. You set per-peer limits, and Circuit
Breaker refuses to forward HTLCs that would saturate a channel and
lock your liquidity.


# CLBoss (/docs/bonus/lightning/clboss)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/lightning/clboss.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[CLBoss](https://github.com/ZmnSCPxj/clboss) is an automated manager
for Core Lightning nodes. It picks useful peers to open channels
with, acquires inbound capacity through Boltz swaps, rebalances
existing channels, and sets competitive forwarding fees, so your
CLN node can run largely on autopilot.


# Core Lightning (/docs/bonus/lightning/cln)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/lightning/cln.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[Core Lightning](https://github.com/ElementsProject/lightning) (CLN)
is a lightweight, highly customizable implementation of the
Lightning Network protocol from Blockstream. You can run it as a
drop-in replacement for LND on your RaspiBolt, or alongside LND so
both implementations share the same `bitcoind`.


# Lightning bonus (/docs/bonus/lightning)



This section gathers Lightning tools that extend what the core
RaspiBolt gives you: browser dashboards, CLI status scripts,
liquidity and fee automation, the Core Lightning implementation as
an alternative or companion to LND, and wallets that talk to your
node over the local network.

<Callout type="info" title="Content being migrated">
  The bonus pages are still being rewritten for v4. Each page links to
  the v3 source while we work through the backlog.
</Callout>

## What's in this section [#whats-in-this-section]

<Cards>
  <Card title="ThunderHub" href="/docs/bonus/lightning/thunderhub">
    Browser-based LND node manager.
  </Card>

  <Card title="LNDg" href="/docs/bonus/lightning/lndg">
    Web GUI for LND routing-node automation.
  </Card>

  <Card title="lntop" href="/docs/bonus/lightning/lntop">
    Interactive text-mode channel viewer.
  </Card>

  <Card title="lnbalance" href="/docs/bonus/lightning/lnbalance">
    Quick CLI balance summary.
  </Card>

  <Card title="lnchannels" href="/docs/bonus/lightning/lnchannels">
    Quick CLI channel summary.
  </Card>

  <Card title="AmbossPing" href="/docs/bonus/lightning/ambossping">
    Health-check pings to amboss.space.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Balance of Satoshis" href="/docs/bonus/lightning/balance-of-satoshis">
    LND power-user toolkit for rebalancing and monitoring.
  </Card>

  <Card title="charge-lnd" href="/docs/bonus/lightning/charge-lnd">
    Policy-based fee manager for LND.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Circuit Breaker" href="/docs/bonus/lightning/circuit-breaker">
    Firewall that protects LND from HTLC-flooding attacks.
  </Card>

  <Card title="rebalance-lnd" href="/docs/bonus/lightning/rebalance-lnd">
    Circular rebalancing CLI for LND.
  </Card>

  <Card title="regolancer" href="/docs/bonus/lightning/regolancer">
    Lightweight LND rebalancer in Go.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Core Lightning" href="/docs/bonus/lightning/cln">
    Alternative Lightning implementation you can run instead of or alongside LND.
  </Card>

  <Card title="LNbits" href="/docs/bonus/lightning/lnbits">
    Lightning wallet and accounts system with extensions.
  </Card>

  <Card title="CLBoss" href="/docs/bonus/lightning/clboss">
    Automated channel and liquidity manager for Core Lightning.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Lightning Terminal" href="/docs/bonus/lightning/lightning-terminal">
    Lightning Labs UI for Loop and Pool.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Zap Desktop" href="/docs/bonus/lightning/zap-desktop">
    Cross-platform desktop Lightning wallet.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Zap (iOS)" href="/docs/bonus/lightning/zap-ios">
    iOS Lightning wallet pointed at your node.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Remote lncli" href="/docs/bonus/lightning/remote-lncli">
    Run lncli from another computer on your network.
  </Card>
</Cards>


# Lightning Terminal (/docs/bonus/lightning/lightning-terminal)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/lightning/lightning-terminal.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[Lightning Terminal](https://github.com/lightninglabs/lightning-terminal)
is a web UI from Lightning Labs that helps LND operators manage
channel liquidity through the commercial Loop and Pool services. It
bundles those services in a single interface and is useful if you
want managed submarine swaps or liquidity leases without writing
scripts.


# lnbalance (/docs/bonus/lightning/lnbalance)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/lightning/lnbalance.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

`lnbalance` is a compact shell script by
[RobClark56](https://github.com/robclark56) that prints a one-screen
balance summary for your Lightning node. It's handy when you want
numbers on-demand from the terminal without waiting on a heavier
dashboard.


# LNbits (/docs/bonus/lightning/lnbits)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/lightning/lnbits.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[LNbits](https://github.com/lnbits/lnbits) is a free and open-source
Lightning wallet and accounts system. It sits on top of your node
and lets you carve out sub-wallets with their own balances, APIs,
and third-party integrations, handy for splitting a family node,
powering a point of sale, or experimenting with LN extensions.


# lnchannels (/docs/bonus/lightning/lnchannels)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/lightning/lnchannels.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

`lnchannels` is a companion script by
[RobClark56](https://github.com/robclark56) that prints a clean
overview of all your Lightning channels and their state. It pairs
well with `lnbalance` for quick, terminal-only status checks on the
node.


# LNDg (/docs/bonus/lightning/lndg)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/lightning/lndg.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[LNDg](https://github.com/cryptosharks131/lndg) is a lightweight web
interface for LND aimed at routing-node operators. It surfaces
channel and forwarding data and can automate rebalancing, fee
adjustments, and channel opens so you spend less time manually
running `lncli` commands.


# lntop (/docs/bonus/lightning/lntop)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/lightning/lntop.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[lntop](https://github.com/edouardparis/lntop) is an interactive,
text-mode channels viewer for Lightning nodes on Unix systems. If
you live in the terminal, it gives you a compact, keyboard-driven
overview of channel state and flow without spinning up a browser.


# rebalance-lnd (/docs/bonus/lightning/rebalance-lnd)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/lightning/rebalance-lnd.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[rebalance-lnd](https://github.com/C-Otto/rebalance-lnd) by Carsten
Otto is a focused CLI tool for circular rebalancing on LND: it sends
sats out of channels with too much outbound liquidity and routes
them back through channels that need inbound. Useful when you want
predictable, scriptable rebalances without a full automation stack.


# regolancer (/docs/bonus/lightning/regolancer)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/lightning/regolancer.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[regolancer](https://github.com/rkfg/regolancer) is a compact LND
rebalancer written in Go. It's quick to set up and runs
efficiently on a Raspberry Pi, making it a good match when you want
routine circular rebalances without the overhead of a larger
automation tool.


# Remote lncli (/docs/bonus/lightning/remote-lncli)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/lightning/remote-lncli.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

This guide shows how to run `lncli` on a different machine on your
network, typically a Windows or Linux desktop, while LND itself
keeps running on the RaspiBolt. You get a responsive CLI without
having to SSH into the node every time you want to inspect or drive
it.


# ThunderHub (/docs/bonus/lightning/thunderhub)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/lightning/thunderhub.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[ThunderHub](https://github.com/apotdevin/thunderhub) is an
open-source LND node manager that runs in your browser. It gives you
an intuitive UI for the full range of node operations, channels,
payments, forwards, backups, accessible from any device on your
network.


# Zap Desktop (/docs/bonus/lightning/zap-desktop)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/lightning/zap-desktop.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[Zap Desktop](https://github.com/LN-Zap/zap-desktop) is a
cross-platform Lightning Network wallet focused on a smooth UX.
Pointing it at your RaspiBolt over gRPC gives you a graphical
desktop wallet that spends and receives using your own channels,
no custodial backend in the middle.


# Zap (iOS) (/docs/bonus/lightning/zap-ios)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/lightning/zap-ios.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[Zap iOS](https://github.com/LN-Zap/zap-iOS) is an iPhone Lightning
wallet that talks to your own LND node over gRPC. It gives you a
clean mobile UI for managing peers, opening channels, making
payments, and generating invoices, all backed by the node running
in your own home.


# Aliases (/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/aliases)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/raspberry-pi/aliases.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

Aliases are shortcuts for long or frequently used shell commands.
The guide installs a curated `.bash_aliases` file so `admin` gets
concise shortcuts for inspecting Bitcoin Core, LND, system logs, and
service status, saving you the muscle memory of typing the full
command every time.


# Boot from microSD card (/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/boot-from-microsd-card)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/raspberry-pi/boot-from-microsd-card.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

Not every Raspberry Pi can boot directly from an external drive. If
yours can't, or your USB-SATA enclosure is picky, this guide sets
up a split configuration: boot the OS from a microSD card, then mount
the external drive for all the application and blockchain data.


# Pimp the command line (/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/command-line)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/raspberry-pi/command-line.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

A small quality-of-life tweak: make your shell prompt shine with a
golden Bitcoin symbol, enable color output for `ls`, and nudge the
defaults so the terminal is easier on the eyes. Purely cosmetic, but
it takes two minutes and you'll appreciate it every time you log
back in.


# Install Go (/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/go)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/raspberry-pi/go.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

A few RaspiBolt bonus tools (regolancer and some monitoring
helpers) are written in [Go](https://go.dev). This short guide handles
installing, upgrading, and removing the Go toolchain on the Pi, so
you can build those tools yourself without polluting the rest of
the system.


# Homer (/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/homer)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/raspberry-pi/homer.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

[Homer](https://github.com/bastienwirtz/homer) is a simple
browser-based dashboard for your self-hosted services. You list your
web tools in a YAML file and Homer renders a clean, bookmarkable
homepage, one click into RTL, ThunderHub, Mempool, BTC RPC
Explorer, or whatever else you've spun up.


# Raspberry Pi bonus (/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi)



This section covers the host system rather than the Bitcoin or
Lightning stack: shell tweaks, dashboards, alternative boot and
hardware configurations, UPS integration, and hardware-wallet-based
SSH. None of it is required for a working node, it's what you
reach for when you're ready to polish the setup.

<Callout type="info" title="Content being migrated">
  The bonus pages are still being rewritten for v4. Each page links to
  the v3 source while we work through the backlog.
</Callout>

## What's in this section [#whats-in-this-section]

<Cards>
  <Card title="System overview" href="/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/system-overview">
    Login dashboard with node and system status.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Aliases" href="/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/aliases">
    Shell shortcuts for common node-management commands.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Pimp the command line" href="/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/command-line">
    Colorful prompt and nicer ls defaults.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Homer" href="/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/homer">
    Browser dashboard with links to all your web services.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Install Go" href="/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/go">
    Install, update, or uninstall the Go toolchain.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Boot from microSD" href="/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/boot-from-microsd-card">
    Boot from microSD and use the external drive for data.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Upgrade external drive" href="/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/upgrade-external-drive">
    Migrate your node data to a larger drive.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Odroid setup" href="/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/odroid-setup">
    Run RaspiBolt on an Odroid XU4 or compatible board.
  </Card>

  <Card title="Trezor SSH agent" href="/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/trezor-agent">
    Use a Trezor as a hardware-backed SSH key.
  </Card>

  <Card title="UPS with NUT" href="/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/ups-nut">
    Graceful shutdown when a connected UPS runs low.
  </Card>
</Cards>


# Odroid setup (/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/odroid-setup)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/raspberry-pi/odroid-setup.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

If you'd rather run RaspiBolt on different hardware, this guide
adapts the setup to the [Odroid XU4](https://www.hardkernel.com/)
and compatible boards like the HC1 and HC2. The main guide's
software choices still apply, only the OS bring-up and a handful
of platform-specific tweaks change.


# System overview (/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/system-overview)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/raspberry-pi/system-overview.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

The [raspibolt-pulse](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt-pulse)
script prints a dense, color-coded status overview on every SSH
login, block height, channel balances, disk usage, memory, and
service health all in one glance. It's the fastest way to confirm
the node is healthy before you start tinkering.


# Trezor SSH agent (/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/trezor-agent)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/raspberry-pi/trezor-agent.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

Instead of trusting a plain SSH key on your laptop, you can let a
Trezor hardware wallet sign SSH challenges. Your key never leaves
the device, and every login prompts you to physically confirm on
the Trezor, a meaningful bump in security for the admin account
that runs your node.


# Upgrade external drive (/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/upgrade-external-drive)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/raspberry-pi/upgrade-external-drive.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

The blockchain keeps growing, so the 1 TB drive that felt generous
three years ago will eventually fill up. This guide walks through
cloning your node's data onto a larger drive and swapping it in
without losing channels or state, minimizing downtime for your
Lightning node along the way.


# UPS with NUT (/docs/bonus/raspberry-pi/ups-nut)



<Callout type="info" title="Migration in progress">
  This bonus page hasn't been rewritten for RaspiBolt v4 yet. The v3
  version still works, see the [original guide on GitHub](https://github.com/raspibolt/raspibolt/blob/master/guide/bonus/raspberry-pi/ups-nut.md)
  while we port it over.
</Callout>

A sudden power loss can corrupt the Bitcoin or LND database and
force a lengthy resync. With a UPS and
[Network UPS Tools](https://networkupstools.org/) (NUT), your node
watches the battery level and triggers a clean shutdown before the
UPS runs out, turning a brownout into a five-minute nuisance
instead of hours of downtime.
